title: This common skincare ingredient halts drug-resistant E. coli
html_body:
Microorganisms in health, the environment and food systems |
View this email in your browser Thursday 16 April 2026

Hello Nature readers,
This week, we learn that a common skincare ingredient can stop resistant bacteria in its tracks, investigate a case of viruses allegedly stolen from a high-security facility and meet the mycologists championing fungal conservation in Africa.

If you enjoy this newsletter, why not recommend it to a friend or colleague? If you know someone with an interest in all things microorganisms, click here to forward the newsletter by e-mail. Thank you!
Madecassic acid is often used in skincare products that soothe eczema and can be beneficial in wound healing. (Sergey Mironov/Getty)

Skincare chemical thwarts resistant E. coli

The plant chemical madecassic acid — a common ingredient in skincare products — can block the growth of drug-resistant strains of Escherichia coli. Researchers found that the molecule blocks the activity of cytochrome bd, a protein complex involved in the bacteria’s energy-making process. The structure of madecassic acid can also be easily modified, which could help scientists optimize its antimicrobial properties, the team says.

The Microbiologist | 5 min read
 Reference: RCS Medicinal Chemistry paper

Lenacapavir to get further-reaching rollout

The US Department of State and The Global Fund have announced joint plans to scale up their rollout of lenacapavir — a treatment that can prevent HIV infection for 6 months with one injection — over the next 3 years. The organizations had previously pledged to make the treatment available to 2 million people across nine African countries. The new commitment pushes the total number of people they aim to reach to 3 million, and expands their support to 15 additional countries, including some in Europe and southeast Asia.

Meanwhile, the humanitarian organization Médecins Sans Frontières is not happy that the US drug company Gilead Sciences, which makes lenacapavir, has turned down its requests to buy more doses on top of what it gets through The Global Fund.

Science | 5 min read & The BMJ | 6 min read

Viruses allegedly stolen from lab in Brazil

A researcher was arrested last month for allegedly taking samples of viruses — reportedly including chikungunya and dengue — from a high-security biosafety laboratory at a leading Brazilian university. The samples were recovered, and were not dangerous, but the event has the Brazilian virology community “perplexed”, says virologist Paulo Sanches. “No sample can be removed from a lab with this biosafety level without authorization.” The news comes as Brazil is building support for the construction of its first BSL-4 lab — the highest level of biosafety facility — only a few kilometres away from the campus where the samples went missing.

Nature | 8 min read

Pesticides might harm our gut microbes

Evidence is growing that pesticides might disrupt the gut microbiome — and could be linked to the global rise of type 2 diabetes. A study in southern India found a surprisingly high prevalence of the disease (16%) in people who lived in the countryside, despite a lack of risk factors such as obesity. And the same team found that, in mice, a popular agricultural insecticide called chlorpyrifos seemed to repress populations of helpful bacteria in favour of potentially harmful ones, and the mice developed diabetes. “It’s not one single mechanism. It’s a network of biological effects that can ultimately disrupt communication along the gut–brain axis,” says neuroscientist John Cryan — but it’s a huge challenge to prove that pesticides are definitely the cause of harm in humans.

Science | 9 min read
 Reference: Diabetic Medicine paper & Environmental Science and Pollution Research paper

Species of the week

Myxococcus llanfairpwllgwyngyllgogerychwyrndro-
bwllllantysiliogogogochensis

This myxobacterium has the longest scientific name of any organism. Researchers have found that its mouthful of a moniker disadvantages the species — and others with long scientific names — when it comes to getting mentions in scientific literature. (Reference: bioarXiv preprint, not peer reviewed)

What do you think is the best microbial species name? Be it outlandish, hiding a pop-culture reference you love or just down-right clever, let us know at microbiology-briefing@nature.com and you might see your picks spotlighted in a future edition of this newsletter.

Features and opinion

Home-grown fungal conservation in Africa

As the first mycologist working in Madagascar who is from the island, Anna Ralaiveloarisoa works to better understand the abundance and variety of fungal species in her home country. She is one of many African scientists stepping up to champion fungal conservation across the continent. “Africa is still finding its way in fungal conservation”, says mycologist Joyce Jefwa, who works in Kenya. But milestones such as the International Congress on Fungal Conservation, which was hosted in Benin last year, indicate that the global scientific community is beginning to recognize Africa’s role in the conservation movement.

The Guardian | 7 min read

Prevent outbreaks, don’t just contain them

Many global-health strategies still focus on responding to outbreaks stemming from animal-to-human transmission, rather than preventing this spillover in the first place, argue a group of health researchers. The group have co-founded PREZODE, an initiative focussed on reducing the root cause of zoonotic spillover. Bringing together international scientists, policymakers and community leaders, PREZODE has drawn up a roadmap that lays out particular areas of focus over the next decade to reduce the threat of zoonoses.

Nature Health | 12 min read

There’s nothing basic about ‘basic research’

Microbial-derived technologies such as CRISPR–Cas9 gene-editing tools and vaccines have transformed entire fields of research. But all of these findings are underpinned by years of basic, discovery-driven science, which can have a less obvious impact on human lives, argues a Nature Microbiology editorial. Environmental microbiology in particular relies on research, which is still only scratching the surface of characterizing the microbes in ecosystems from deserts to geysers. These microbes could have properties that are extremely useful to humans, but “this potential cannot be harnessed if basic biology remains uncharacterized”, says the editorial.

Nature Microbiology | 6 min read

Image of the week
Swimming E. coli can spin a microscopic ‘puck’ without ever actually touching it. Researchers sandwiched the bacteria between a pane of glass and a specially-printed disc that contained rows of channels. When a bacterium swims into one of these channels, its cell body stirs the fluid at one point and its flagellum — a corkscrew-shaped tail that the bacterium uses to move — at another point, generating torque that helps to spin the entire puck. This film has been sped up 10 times. (Nature Research Highlight | 3 min read)
Reference: Nature Physics paper (Grober et al./Nat. Phys.)

Quote of the week

“The moment you clash with cultures, that policy doesn’t work.”

Forty-one percent of wild mammalian species traded for food, fur, research and traditional medicines carry at least one pathogen that causes disease in humans, a new study estimates. But the solution is not to ban this trade, which is common in many cultures, says virologist Arinjay Banerjee. Instead, he recommends asking communities to improve hygiene and safety at wildlife markets. (Nature | 4 min read)
Reference: Science paper

Long-time subscribers to this newsletter might remember Jackie and Shadow, two bald eagles (Haliaeetus leucocephalus) that nest in southern California. Last year, I gave you play-by-play updates as they raised two chicks, named Gizmo and Sunny by local schoolkids. A year on, I’m delighted to learn that the pair’s two newest chicks hatched in the first few days of April.

The eaglets are still too small for researchers to guess their sex, at which point schoolchildren will be given their usual chance to pick names. Let me know if you have any early predictions, along with your feedback on this newsletter, at microbiology-briefing@nature.com.

Thanks for reading,
Jacob Smith, associate editor, Nature Briefing
With contributions by Flora Graham
